1: Anyone — human or agent — can stand up the project from a clean machine and arrive at a working state without tribal knowledge.
2: Mistakes are caught before they reach review. Static analysis runs locally and in CI on identical configuration.
3: The shape of the codebase is legible. Modules have stated responsibilities and known import boundaries — both humans and agents can reason about scope.
4: Verification cannot be skipped. The same checks run locally before commit and on every push.
5: Secrets never live in the repository, in chat with an agent, or in build artifacts.
6: Third-party code is treated as untrusted input. Known vulnerabilities are surfaced and acted on.
7: The build pipeline itself is a privileged surface. It runs untrusted code only with intent.
8: Privileged operations are gated by explicit authorization checks at the boundary, not at the call site.
9: Security is reasoned about explicitly, not inferred. Trust boundaries are written down.
10: Inputs at process boundaries are validated. Bad data is rejected loudly, not propagated.
11: There is a stated testing strategy, and CI runs the tests it implies. Tests are fast enough to be run on every change.
12: User-facing surfaces are usable by people with disabilities. Accessibility is part of verification, not a follow-up.
13: When things break, the system says so. Logs, metrics, and traces are deliberate, not accidental.
14: Performance and reliability targets are stated, measured, and defended.
15: What ships is what was reviewed. Provenance is verifiable.
16: The default branch is sacred. Releases happen through documented rituals, not by accident.
17: Agents need a README written for them. AGENTS.md is the entry point: how to run, test, and contribute, in machine-readable form.
18: Recurring agent workflows live in the repo, not in chat history.
19: Agents only get the tools they're meant to have. Tool catalogs are explicit and auditable.
20: Agents that act without per-step human approval are first-class subjects: they have identities, scopes, and audit trails.
21: Anything an agent reads can become an instruction. Untrusted sources are gated and marked.
22: Sensitive data does not leak into model providers, telemetry, or logs without intent.
23: At every level above L1, humans hold the gate. What requires approval, and from whom, is written down.
24: Claims about what agents can and cannot do are backed by evidence in the repository.
25: Someone, by name or role, owns the AI policy. It is not orphaned.
26: AI-authored work is attributed and licensed clearly.
27: Credentials issued to agents are short-lived, scoped, and revocable.
28: Switching the model is a code change, not a config tweak slipped past review.
29: AI-driven incidents are first-class. There is a known way to stop, investigate, and learn from them.
